I guess that\'s the whole point, right? So, fundamentals of AI

responding felt stuck. It was funny. Almost everyone was using ChatGPT

through the next level, or that they\'re overwhelmed by the options
available to them. And so I submit to you that you do not have a. You
don\'t have a technology problem. You have a. Or a tactics problem. You
have a mental model problem.

**\
06:34**\
Vrijen Attawar\
Right?

**\
06:35**\
Vrijen Attawar\
It\'s not that you lack the tools or even the knowledge, it\'s that. And
what we\'ve sort of observed from folks, that it feels like you\'re
collecting individual techniques without actually understanding how they
fit, right? Without understanding how they sort of bind with each other
and create sort of outcomes or create outputs or anything that is better
than average. Which is another problem folks face, is that a lot of
their AI content or their output seems to come off as bang average. So
this wasn\'t just meant to be a extremely cringy tech bro thing to say
AI is magic. It is the idea of collapsing the gap between intent and
outcome.

**\
07:13**\
Vrijen Attawar\
And so when I say AI is magic, I essentially mean that we are reducing
the Gap between maybe a thousand years ago when you said, hey, I want
some food, I have to grow it in the fields. Now you doordash and your
mental model of the steps needed to make that happen has changed. It\'s
not magic, although it would have seemed magic to someone a thousand
years ago that someone shows up in your burrito taxi. But it, for all
intents and purposes is because we have the mental model to map that
onto reality. And that\'s all this is. You\'ve probably heard the term
context engineering and the idea is that you don\'t prompt the AI per
se. You work with the AI to cultivate the context that it then uses to
collapse down to the output that you want. Right?

**\
07:59**\
Vrijen Attawar\
So it\'s almost like you\'re sort of priming up the pump, right? Or
you\'re loading up the AI with all the informational goodness that it
needs to do its job. And there are essentially three levels of context
that I would encourage you to think about, right? Or sort of like
adaptations that you can make. There\'s the stuff that you can do in a
conversation or at the conversation level.

**\
08:26**\
Vrijen Attawar\
There\'s stuff that you can do at.

**\
08:27**\
Vrijen Attawar\
The environmental level or the environment level, product level, and
then there\'s the pipeline level where you bring in the external world
into whatever AI system that you are using or deploying, right? And bear
in mind, I\'m saying AI system, I\'m not referring to this idea of
disparate tools. I\'m truly trying to work us towards this idea of a
coherent system.

**\
08:51**\
Yixian Zheng\
Right?

**\
08:51**\
Vrijen Attawar\
So how do we get to that system? And no shame in which level you are at
this point. Everyone starts at some level and the reality is that we
would all do well to incorporate these, these tactics into our, in our
day to day. The other thing you\'ll notice is that as we move up levels,
it goes from sort of tactical do this, do that to more strategic. This
is how you approach it. That is both intentional and just the way of
cognitive complexity. So yeah, to start off with conversation
engineering, some really basic things to do. Don\'t ask for the output
to early. Right. Again, cultivate the context, ask it for where to look.
Encourage the AI to look in particular ways.

**\
09:30**\
Vrijen Attawar\
If it\'s going to do research, give it advice or information on how to

Vrijen Attawar\

threshold rubric, right? Asking the AI for any given task. Hey, think
through. If you were going to evaluate this, what would be a rubric you

me against that rubric or iterate on the output until you reach the

Mine is more around building a year of context and memory in ChatGPT.

continue to work with it. It\'s one of the things that\'s made it hard
to go to other systems and try other systems because you get all this
valuable data that\'s stranded in one. So I was wondering what your
thoughts were around that.

**\
41:09**\
Vrijen Attawar\
So I would basic. I would basically construct a prompt that. Two types
of prompts. One prompt that basically is geared towards digging into
your explicit or stored memory, capturing everything that it can. My gut
feeling has always been that there exists information that ChatGPT has
that isn\'t an explicit memory. But when I delete explicit memory and it
still remembers things about me, somehow I\'m supposed to be like, you
know, not shocked by that. But long story short, building a prompt that
tells it to look inwards, telling it where to look within explicit
memory, telling it what categories of information to pay attention to,
and then just running that in. And this is the trick, running it in deep
research and at least in ChatGPT, turning off the web search.

**\
41:57**\
Vrijen Attawar\
So there\'s a way that you can do that where you basically get it to do
deep research, but it isn\'t searching the web. And what it will do is
it will reapply that same agentic sort of intensity to deep researching
you and your chat GPT information. So that\'s actually, I\'m especially
qualified to answer that because I had to figure that out to move all of
my stuff to zo. So if you can actually build out a good prompt that
excavates that stuff from your chat GPT, you can get pretty far.

42:25**\
Benjamin Goldstein\

that really levels you up productivity wise. Thank you. Thank you, Ben.

using it from your vantage point as someone that\'s like a pretty senior

52:47**\

Yeah, totally. I can just present some slides super quickly that I use

Kind of insane, you know, I\'m spending like thousands of dollars on
tokens, even though they\'re highly subsidized every month. And it\'s
just my personal spend anyway. I have a lot of things that I\'m doing
besides coding, but I\'m still able to keep my velocity really high. And
basically the way that I think about this is this model of foreground
task background tasks. And I have a main foreground task that\'s the
bulk of my attention. I\'m planning a lot, being very rigorous and
intentional. And then I have these really small one shot means I know
that I can fix them with just a simple prompt. I have those tasks
running in the background.

**\
53:31**\
Ben Guo\
And that\'s kind of like my model switching between the foreground task

creating these ideas that go into my kind of background tasks backlog.
And there\'s kind of like degrees of difficulty of these tasks that go
in the background task queue. And the really easy ones, I\'ll just prop
my Zoe so I\'ll fix it. It\'ll make a pull request and I\'ll review it
later. Then kind of more complex ones, sometimes I\'m writing plans in

telling your AI where stuff is and then giving it good guidance on how
to mash that together in a way that is unique to your thinking and your
